data.title: Important Warning For Utility Stocks: 2 Major Risks You Need To See Before Buying
data.description:
Utilities (XLU) have been on a phenomenal run recently, thanks in large part to the ongoing AI boom, which is expected to drive significant demand for additional electric generation, as well as growing concerns about a recession in the United States.

As you can see in the chart below, since last July, they have significantly outperformed the S&P 500, as capital has flowed into the sector over investor optimism about its growth potential as well as its safe haven status due to the regulated nature of utility assets in the face of a potential economic downturn in the wake of the ongoing trade war.

That being said, there are several reasons why the recent strong run of utility stocks may not last. In this article, we will detail why.

Important Warning #1

The first reason is simply valuation-related. As we pointed out earlier in this article, utilities have been on a strong run and have significantly outperformed the broader market. However, as a result, they now trade at one of their lowest dividend yields sector-wide over the past five years, despite long-term interest rates rising significantly over that period of time.

This leads to the next risk-simply the fact that, given their valuation is not low, there is not much margin of safety against potential interest rate risk. The utility sector therefore remains very interest rate sensitive. If inflation indeed does remain sticky due to tariffs or other causes, the sector could potentially see a meaningful pullback as long-term interest rates remain higher for longer or potentially even move higher from here.

Important Warning #2

Another major risk that has recently been highlighted by Warren Buffett of Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B) in his 2024 meeting is that utilities remain very exposed to natural disaster risk, as has been experienced by Hawaiian Electric (HE), as well as risks to California utilities like Edison International (EIX). Therefore the recent major natural disaster in Hawaii, as well as consistent risks of wildfires in California-and the more liberal, anti-business bent of both of those states-tends to give them an unfavorable risk-reward profile. This leads to a fundamental issue that I do have with the regulated utilities business model: namely, that while the regulated nature of their cash flows does create a bond-like structure, they are still very much common equity investments and therefore suffer extreme downside risk in the event of a major natural disaster. Meanwhile, their upside is capped-much like a bond's is-due to the regulatory nature that they have. Sure, they do have the growth component that can and does enable them to outperform bonds over the long term, but on a risk-adjusted basis, the upside potential is capped while the downside risk is still fully in place.

This especially has proven to be the case because their ability to pass on increased costs is limited by regulatory bodies. As a result, if they are in a jurisdiction that is not pro-business and is much more likely to cave to political pressure about keeping costs low for customers-especially residential customers-their risk-reward becomes very unfavorable, and they can quickly become a scapegoat for politicians and people in general who are looking for someone to blame or even to shoulder the vast majority of the financial burden of a natural disaster or a highly inflationary environment.

This becomes especially important in the current economic environment where input costs may go up due to tariffs on imports even at the same time as skilled labor supply and demand for significant growth in capital investments soar due to a potential manufacturing renaissance as well as the AI boom in the United States. Therefore, utilities may be caught in a place where their spending soars due to higher labor costs, higher input costs, and a significant increase in capital projects to try to keep up with demand at the same time as their cost of capital may rise due to elevated interest rates, just as their ability to pass through cost increases to customers may be limited by regulatory bodies that are hesitant to further raise living costs on customers already being hit by inflationary trends across the economy.

Investor Takeaway

When you piece together the elevated valuations and the unfavorable risk-reward factor that comes with being regulated, utilities are not nearly as attractive as they may first appear on paper when thinking about them as a safe-haven from the growing risks of a recession in the near term as well as the long-term tailwind of the AI boom. This is primarily due to the fact that they are, at least to some degree, at the mercy of the regulatory agencies, while still being exposed to numerous downside risks, including natural disasters as well as potential significant input cost increases due to a convergence of macroeconomic factors.

That said, we are not totally abandoning the sector, as we do like infrastructure in general a lot right now and utilities can offer an attractive combination of attractive dividend yield, strong growth, and relatively low risk. However, we are being very selective in where we invest-either choosing the few remaining utilities that are still undervalued, such as ATCO (OTCPK:ACLLF) / Canadian Utilities (OTCPK:CDUAF), which still have attractive long-term growth potential, solid current yields, and strong balance sheets while trading at a steeply discounted EV/EBITDA valuation relative to peers like Fortis (FTS) and others.

Another approach we like is investing in well-managed diversified infrastructure CEFs that have significant exposure to utilities stocks balanced out with other infrastructure holdings and trade at reasonable valuations relative to their underlying NAVs (and roughly in line with their historical price-to-NAV ratios) and offer attractive yields, like the Reaves Utility Income Trust (UTG) and the Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF).

Finally, we look for hybrid business models that incorporate a core utilities element along with growthier opportunities that help to diversify some of the risks and also provide higher yields and overall growth potential, and therefore better risk-adjusted total returns. For example, Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC) and Enbridge (ENB) are two great examples of this, as they both own meaningful utilities businesses but also have other infrastructure businesses that combine to provide a better overall risk-reward profile for investors. This is the sort of approach we are taking in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: TransMedics: Exceptional Q1 Undermines The Short Narrative
data.description:
TransMedics's (TMDX) Q1 results were extremely strong across the board, which is no surprise for anyone that has been tracking transplant volumes and flight activity. While growth could exhibit volatility from quarter to quarter, and competition will increase in the near future, many of the concerns that have been weighing on the stock have been put to bed.

I previously suggested that the market had placed too little faith in TransMedics’ guidance, which has likely been in part due to the uncertainty created by a short attack in January. The most salient points raised were related to pricing and competition, but continued market share gains should begin to put these fears to bed. While I tend to think that TransMedics' long-term targets could be overly aggressive, the company's share price should still be higher at this point.

Market Conditions

Concerns about the strength of TransMedics' business arose after a weak third quarter in 2024, which saw soft growth in transplant volumes and more moderate market share gains for TransMedics. These trends began to reverse in early 2025 though, and data currently points towards strong growth again in Q2. As a result, it is not really surprising that TransMedics reported strong results in Q1 and raised full-year guidance. The results should also help to allay concerns around the threat from NRP and the pricing and efficacy of TransMedics' services.

On the tariff front, TransMedics has stated that it is a US manufacturer and that the tariffs currently proposed will have little impact on its business. The fact that organ transplantation is a life-saving procedure should also help to limit the impact of macro volatility. Having said this, transplant volumes in the US have historically been correlated with economic growth, meaning a recession could cause TransMedics' growth to slow.

TransMedics job openings continue to increase at a healthy pace, which supports the notion of ongoing demand growth and expansion of TransMedics' business in 2025.

TransMedics Business Updates

Daily aircraft availability averaged 15.4 in the first quarter, up from 14 in the fourth quarter of 2024. These aircraft provided 78% NOP coverage, up from 75% in Q4. TransMedics' fleet currently contains 21 aircraft and is expected to grow to 22 before year-end. The company is targeting 18-20 operational aircraft on average and also wants to double shift around 20-30% of its aircraft, significantly increasing its capacity.

In addition to expanding its logistics capabilities, TransMedics wants to support growth by introducing new devices that strengthen its heart and lungs business. While TransMedics has a reasonably strong heart transplant business, it has so far struggled to gain traction in lung transplants. TransMedics has filed both its OCS Lung and OCS Heart IDEs and is currently discussing details with the FDA. Both programs are expected to launch in the second half of 2025 and become growth drivers in 2026. These will be relatively large trials and will place TransMedics' perfusion devices head-to-head with standard of care approaches.

While TransMedics' business is currently thriving, it will likely face increased competition in the near future, with several competitors expected to enter the market later this year, including Organ Recovery Systems' LifePort Liver Transporter (hypothermic perfusion device). OrganOx's metra device already has FDA clearance, although adoption isn't clear. While some competitors offer less expensive alternatives, competition won't be based solely on price and TransMedics has suggested that even if competing devices were made available for free, its devices would still offer more value.

TransMedics also announced that it plans on opening a design center of excellence and a new disposables manufacturing facility in Italy. Mirandola is known for its expertise in polycarbonate injection molding and disposable perfusion technologies. This is important, as TransMedics' main product is a disposable unit made from polycarbonate and polyurethane. The move suggests that TransMedics could be considering in-sourcing the production of this component in the future. TransMedics hasn't really provided insight into the thought process behind opening a facility in Italy. It will presumably reduce costs or enable the company to introduce new capabilities though.

Financial Analysis

TransMedics' generated $143.5 million of revenue in Q1, an increase of roughly 48% YoY. Growth was strong across products and services, with product growth driven by liver transplants. Service revenue growth was driven by logistics, which grew 80% YoY on the back of a larger aviation fleet and improved aircraft utilization. Given commentary around aircraft plans, I can see logistics revenue increasing by upwards of 50%, although it will still be a relatively small part of the business.

TransMedics now expects $565 to $585 million of revenue in 2025, representing 30% growth at the midpoint. While growth could exhibit some quarterly variability, this guidance appears to be overly conservative. Overall transplant volume growth in the US is robust at the moment, and TransMedics continues to gain market share. In addition, TransMedics' clinical trials are expected to commence later in the year, potentially providing a 2-5% boost to annual revenue. These trials won't just be cannibalizing existing heart and lung volumes either. Part of TransMedics' heart trial is targeting a new indication, and this represents the majority of planned patient volumes (750 patients for the new indication versus 200 patients for the current indication). As a result, I would not be surprised to see full-year revenue come in above $600 million.

TransMedics' gross margin was 61.5% in Q1, up sequentially but down YoY. While service revenue growth is a gross margin headwind, the first quarter didn't see any inventory-related charges and had better fixed cost absorption. TransMedics' service gross margin improved from 29.2% in Q4 2024 to 29.6% in the first quarter of 2025. While aircraft maintenance may introduce some margin volatility in coming quarters, service margins are generally expected to continue improving.

TransMedics net income totaled $25.7 million in Q1 at roughly an 18% margin. Operating expenses increased 28% YoY, driven by R&D investments. The first quarter also saw TransMedics incur legal and consulting expenses related to an internal review that came after January's short attack. While TransMedics' business is now profitable, cash flows are currently depressed as a result of aircraft purchases. These are likely to moderate going forward, which should see TransMedics begin to generate fairly healthy free cash flows.

TransMedics now expects its operating margin to improve by 4% in 2025, suggesting roughly a 12% operating margin. This would represent a material decline from Q1 levels, driven by investments in the company's product portfolio.

Conclusion

Investors and analysts over-extrapolated temporary headwinds in late 2024 and have been slow to recognize these headwinds have abated. For example, analysts were only projecting $540 million of revenue in 2025, but TransMedics now appears well positioned to exceed $600 million in revenue. The company also retains its 10,000 NOP transplant target in the U.S. for 2028, which would require close to a 30% CAGR from 2024's volumes. International markets and additional organs could also provide meaningful upside to what is already an aggressive growth target.

While TransMedics' share price has rebounded somewhat, I believe the company's fundamentals justify a larger market capitalization. The appropriate revenue multiple for the company at this point in time is heavily dependent on how sustainable its growth rate is. The next hurdle facing the company in this regard is the increase in liver perfusion device competition expected in the coming years. Liver growth in the US will also normalize at some point, and TransMedics will eventually cease gaining market share. This makes efforts to gain share in heart and lungs important, with upcoming clinical trials providing more clarity on how successful TransMedics will be.

data.title: Intel: Too Slow, Too Late
data.description:
In the last decade, Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) has lost its dominance over the semiconductor market and has been struggling to execute a proper turnaround lately. It’s still trading around the same price at which it was trading in December when we released our latest bearish article on the business. Despite the ongoing restructuring efforts, we believe that Intel remains a risky investment. We also continue to rate it as a SELL as the rising competition along with the increasing macroeconomic risks are unlikely to help the stock recover anytime soon.

The Future Is Uncertain

Last month Intel released its Q1 FY25 earnings report, which was relatively weak. The company made only $12.7 billion in revenues, and its gross margin decreased by 6% from 45% to 39%. In Q2, the company expects a revenue of only between $11.2 billion and $12.4 billion. The gross margin is also expected to be only 36.5%, which indicates a further deterioration of the business’s efficiency.

There are some good things happening though. Intel’s management is still trying to execute a turnaround and improve profitability. The new CEO is aiming to limit CapEx and OpEx expenses to make the company lose less money and improve the efficiency of its investments. Currently, Intel is planning to reduce CapEx by $2 billion to $18 billion and limit operating expenses to $17 billion in FY25 and $16 billion in FY26.

However, despite some efforts to restructure the business, Intel’s revenue continues to decline for now while its EBIT margin has deteriorated significantly compared to historical levels. Considering that the company’s Q2 guidance suggests a further decline in margins, we might assume that the recovery is unlikely to happen anytime soon. There’s also a risk that the ongoing trade war and Trump’s tariff policy might pressure margins even more later this year.

Also, while Intel is trying to reduce capital expenditures, they remain significant for now. Although the company is aiming to expand its production capacity, relatively high spending still puts pressure on its ability to generate free cash flow. As the FCF remains negative, debt levels are rising, and Fitch Ratings hints at a possible downgrade if financial performance does not improve, it’s hard to be bullish about Intel at this stage.

In addition to that, the possibility of gaining a market share during the ongoing AI boom is also limited. Intel is steadily losing the CPU market to AMD, and its prospects of capturing an AI and high-performance computing market are questionable due to Nvidia’s dominance. Its foundry business is also lagging behind TSMC and is unlikely to achieve a significant scale or profitability in the foreseeable future.

As such, we don’t think that the future is bright for the company for now. Intel already lost a third of its value in the last year, and considering the rise of macro risks that include Trump’s latest tariff policy and his trade war with China, the future for Intel will likely remain uncertain. Also, Beijing’s potential further retaliation against the United States might affect Intel’s overseas revenues over time as China remains an important semiconductor market right now.

The Real Value of Intel

Considering all of that, we updated our previous valuation model by incorporating key adjustments and revised forecasts based on Intel’s expected performance in the near term.

The updated model assumes a perpetual growth rate of 3%, which remains our standard approach when valuing business under current market conditions. The model was created when Intel was trading at $20.51 per share. The balance sheet data has been taken from the latest earnings report.

The discount rate in the model is 6.44%. We calculate it by figuring out Intel's cost of equity and cost of debt. To calculate the cost of debt, we divided the company’s TTM interest expense by its total debt. To calculate the cost of equity, we used the CAPM model using a risk-free rate is 4.21%, a beta of 1.32, and a market return rate of 7.77%.

When it comes to forecasting Intel’s growth, our sales and EBIT assumptions are not far away from the company’s guidance and market analysts' estimates for the next 5 years. In the model, we also used a 15% tax rate, which we used in our previous models for the semiconductor industry. All the other assumptions are close to the historical performance.

Under those assumptions, our model shows that Intel’s enterprise value is $94.11 billion. Intel’s equity value was calculated by combining the enterprise value with available cash and cash equivalents and subtracting the outstanding total debt. In our case, Intel’s equity value is $52.91 billion. After that, we divided the company’s equity value by the number of its outstanding shares and figured out that Intel’s intrinsic value is $12.18 per share, which is approximately 40.6% below the current market price.

Risks To Our Bearish Thesis

Some upside for Intel still remains. In March, the company announced the appointment of a new CEO – Lip-Bu Tan, a technology leader with deep semiconductor industry expertise. Intel is signaling a renewed commitment to transformation and trying hard to avoid repeating the mistakes of the past. Currently, the management is actively focusing on cost optimization, operational efficiency, and technological reimagining. The latest sale of non-core assets under the new leadership team seems to be in line with the new commitment. There are now also ambitious plans for the foundry business, aimed at making Intel the leading semiconductor manufacturer in the West.

Amid rising tensions between the US and China, Intel could also benefit from “offshoring” initiatives, government subsidies, and policies that encourage domestic semiconductor production. Programs like the CHIPS Act could offer financial support and protect Intel from the supply chain disruption caused by macro events.

Final Thoughts

Right now, Intel is at a turning point. Either the company executes a proper turnaround and emerges stronger from the current crisis, or it once again fails and likely ends up like Yahoo, Sears, Kodak and other once mighty but now failed businesses. While we wish Intel the best, its deteriorating financial performance, operational inefficiencies, and fierce competition make us believe that it nevertheless remains uninvestable for now. That is why we continue to rate Intel as a SELL.

data.title: American Tower: Doubling Down On Its Data Center Ambitions - Maintain Buy
data.description:
AMT Remains Attractively Valued Despite The Ongoing Recovery, As Data Center Continues To Drive Growth

We previously covered American Tower Corporation (AMT) in February 2025, discussing how the new management had delivered sustained organic growth, expanded profit margins, and healthier balance sheet as promised.

Combined with the robust performance metrics, the promising FY2025 guidance, the long-awaited dividend hike, and the ongoing generative AI/data center boom, we had reiterated our Buy rating then.

AMT 1Y Stock Price

Since then, AMT has further rallied upwards with a similar recovery also observed in its REIT peers in varying degrees, with much of their tailwinds attributed to the promising results observed in the semiconductor/data center/telecom sector during the early Q1 '25 earnings season.

These have already signaled the durability of the AI and data center related boom, as numerous hyperscalers, including Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), and Meta (META), reiterated/raised their FY2025 data center CapEx plans in their recent earning calls.

The same promising sentiments have also been observed in the foundry/data center infrastructure stocks, including Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), Vertiv Holdings (VRT), and Celestica Inc. (CLS), with it underscoring why the recent market pessimism surrounding the AI bubble has been overly done.

A similarly strong "demand for data center capacity" has been reported by Digital Realty Trust (DLR) as well, as observed in the REIT's growing bookings and higher backlog through 2027.

This is on top of the robust telecom tailwinds, thanks to the ongoing 5G upgrades/intensified telecom capex through 2026 in the US, as similarly highlighted by the numerous telecom providers, including AT&T (T) and Verizon (VZ) in their recent earning calls.

These promising developments have already been embedded in AMT's robust FQ1 '25 performance metrics, with total revenues of $2.56B (+0.7% QoQ/+1.9% YoY) and Organic Tenant Billings Growth by +4.7% YoY (compared to a year ago at +5.4%).

This is on top of the expanding adj EBITDA margins of 68.1% (+1.6 points QoQ/+2.8 YoY/+5.6 from FY2019 levels of 62.5%) and AFFO per share of $2.75 (+18.5% QoQ/+6.5% YoY).

Given the intensified data center/telecom activities in the US, it is unsurprising that AMT has already reported UCAN region's organic tenant billing growth by +5% YoY in FQ1 '25 (compared to +5.5% in FQ1 '24).

This is on top of the excellent ground-based/rooftop sites demand observed in the EU, the ongoing upgrading in telecom networks in Brazil, and the improved consumer pricing strategies in Nigeria, amongst others, as the international markets deliver accelerated organic tenant billing growth by +6.7% YoY in FQ1 '25 (compared to +6.5% in FQ1 '24).

At the same time, readers must note the accretive impact of the CoreSite data center acquisition on AMT's core telecom offerings, with driving its vertically integrated interconnection "ecosystems across a diverse set of cloud, network, and enterprise customers in leading markets."

This is on top of the fact that the REIT has reported "property revenue in our data center business grew by approximately 9%," accelerated compared to its overall property revenue growth at an FX-neutral rate of +5.9% YoY.

These reasons are also why the management has further doubled down on its data center acquisitions, recently through the DE1 data center purchase in Denver, the US, allowing them to grow their data center and interconnection capabilities ahead.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons are also why AMT has felt confident to raise its FY2025 adj EBITDA guidance to $6.92B (+1.6% YoY) and AFFO per share to $10.44 at the midpoint (+4.8% YoY), compared to the original numbers of $6.89B (+1.1% YoY) and $10.40 (+4.4% YoY), respectively, with it underscoring why the ongoing 5G/data center boom has been a boon.

The same has been observed in the consensus raised forward estimates, with the REIT now expected to report an accelerated top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +3.9%/+4.3% through FY2027, respectively.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +3.8%/+2.08%, albeit decelerating compared to the 5Y historical growth at a CAGR of +6%/+7.4% and the 10Y historical growth at +9.5%/+10.3%, respectively, as the AMT management focuses on its balance sheet deleveraging after the CoreSite acquisition in 2021.

Despite the still elevated total debt levels of $36.86B in FQ1 '25 (-6.1% YoY/-14.7% from FY2021 levels of $43.25B/+65.7% from FY2019 levels of $24.05B), it goes without saying that the REIT's richer profitability has directly contributed to its still stable net-debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 5x (inline YoY/down from 6.8x in FY2021/near to 4.6x in FY2019), with it underscoring their excellent execution across debt refinancing and risk management thus far.

Even so, AMT investors must also temper their intermediate term expectations, since the expensive borrowing cost environment remains a headwind to its growth through acquisition strategy, as observed in its decelerating growth profile.

This is especially since the intensified tariff war is likely to trigger a higher for longer inflationary pressures, with the same hawkish commentaries already offered by the Fed Chair Jerome Powell, with "inflation is pushed higher by tariffs while growth and potentially, employment, weaken."

AMT Valuations

These reasons are also why we believe AMT remains compelling at FWD Price/AFFO valuations of 21.24x, down from the recent heights of 22.94x and the 5Y mean of 23.09x, while nearing the 10Y mean of 21.82x.

When compared to its data center REIT peers, including Equinix, Inc. (EQIX) at FWD Price/AFFO valuations of 23.06x, Iron Mountain Incorporated (IRM) at 19.43x, and Digital Realty Trust, Inc. (DLR) at 25.53x, along with its telecom REIT peer, Crown Castle Inc. (CCI) at 25.48x, it goes without saying that AMT remains reasonably valued at current levels, with it offering interested investors with an excellent margin of safety.

So, Is AMT Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

AMT 5Y Stock Price

For now, AMT has already recovered by +7.2% from the recent April 2025 floor, or by +23.6% from the January 2025 bottom, as the stock remains well-supported at its uptrend line since early 2025.

Despite the rally, the stock continues to trade near to our fair value estimates of $211.50 offered in our last article, based on the FY2024 AFFO per share of $9.96 and the FWD Price/AFFO valuations of 21.24x.

Based on the consensus raised FY2027 AFFO per share estimates from $11.66 to $11.94, there remains a decent upside potential of +15.5% to our raised long-term price target of $253.60 as well.

In addition, AMT has delivered its dividend hike by +4.9% to a quarterly sum of $1.70 per share as promised, matching our prior estimates of FY2025 dividend payout of $6.80 per share as discussed in our last article, while building upon the 5Y dividend growth by +10.6%.

While its recent rally has already triggered the lower forward dividend yields to 3.04%, down from the December 2023 peaks of 4%, we are not overly concerned indeed, given the expansion from its 5Y yield averages of 2.44%.

As a result of the still robust dual-pronged returns across capital appreciation and dividend incomes, we are reiterating our Buy rating for the AMT stock here.

data.title: Metsera: A Must-Add To Your Watchlist Thanks To GLP-1 Obesity Promise
data.description:
Investment Overview - Metsera Latest To Join Obesity Market Challengers

Obesity is forecast by some analysts to become a >$200bn market, with up to 16 new drugs on the market by 2031. Eli Lilly (LLY) and Novo Nordisk (NVO) lead the way in this market, their glucagon-like peptide ("GLP-1") therapies Zepbound and Wegovy generating >$13bn of revenues last year.

Naturally, a host of other companies are targeting the space, including many "Big Pharma" companies, e.g., Amgen (AMGN), Pfizer (PFE), Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY), and Merck (MRK), and smaller biotechs like Structure Therapeutics (GPCR), Tern Pharmaceuticals (TERN), Altimmune (ALT), Viking Therapeutics (VKTX), MBX Biosciences (MBX), Zealand Pharma, and most recently, newly listed Metsera, Inc. (MTSR) - the subject of this post.

Metsera completed its IPO in January this year, raising ~$275m at $18 per share, valuing the company at ~$1.8bn. As with most biotech IPO's, shares dipped in value post-IPO after an initial surge, sinking <$13 last month, but have been buoyant since, rising to $25 per share at the time of writing.

Here is a company description as taken from the 2024 annual report/10K submission:

We are a clinical-stage biotechnology company developing next-generation injectable and oral nutrient stimulated hormone ("NuSH") analog peptides to treat obesity, overweight and related conditions.

Obesity and overweight are among the fastest-growing and most prevalent chronic human conditions, affecting approximately 2.5 billion people worldwide and driving a broad range of severe diseases.

We use our proprietary MINT peptide library of NuSH analog peptides alongside our Half-life Augmentation by Lipid Optimization ("HALO") half-life extending platform and our MOMENTUM oral NuSH analog peptide delivery platform to deliver highly differentiated product candidates that we are advancing into clinical trials for obesity or overweight.

MET-097i, our most advanced product candidate, is a fully biased, monthly, subcutaneously injectable ultra-long acting GLP-1 RA that we are developing for the treatment of obesity and overweight. We have completed a Phase 1/2 clinical trial of MET-097i in the United States in participants who are obese or overweight but otherwise healthy.

Lead Candidate MET-097i Showing Early Promise

Preliminary data from Metsera's Phase 1/2 study of MET-097i showed "mean placebo-adjusted body weight of "11.3% after twelve weekly doses of 1.2 mg and 7.2% after twelve weekly doses of 0.6 mg." The company adds:

The five cohorts that received a potential monthly dose that was four-fold higher than the weekly dose that the participants had received in prior weeks all showed continued weight loss for four weeks after the last dose, for an arithmetic mean placebo-adjusted change from baseline in body weight of 14.2% at day 115 in the group that received a 4.8 mg dose (after receiving 12 weekly doses of 1.2 mg).

A Phase 2b study of the candidate in participants with obesity but without Type 2 diabetes is underway, with data expected in "mid-2025," and a Phase 2b in patients with diabetes is also ongoing, with no specific timeframe for data.

MET-097i is subcutaneously injected like Zepbound and Wegovy, however these drugs require weekly dosing, hence Metsera's candidate has a potential competitive edge, given it may be dosed monthly.

In their respective pivotal clinical studies, Wegovy achieved mean weight loss of 14.9%, and Tirzepatide 21%, at 68 weeks and 72 weeks. This is a high bar - once widely believed to be impossible to achieve by the scientific community - and with few adverse safety events, so it would be premature to suggest Metsera can match or exceed this in later stage studies.

Other Candidates

Besides MET-097i, MET-233i is an amylin targeting candidate (a similar mechanism of action, or "MoA" to Novo's drug candidate Cagrisema) which is in a Phase 1 study. Metsera says, "we are developing MET-233i to be used with MET-097i as a once-weekly or once-monthly combination therapy." There are also two orally administered candidates, MET-224o and MET-097.

In recent weeks, Pharma giant Pfizer has announced that it will shelve the development of its oral GLP-receptor agonist ("GLP-RA") danuglipron, which partly explains why Metsera's share price has been gaining.

With that said, Eli Lilly has reported that its own oral candidate, orforglipron, successfully completed a Phase 3 study and will be submitted for commercial approval. Not to be outdone, Novo Nordisk has announced it has submitted oral Wegovy for approval to the Food and Drug Agency ("FDA"). For contracting reasons, the potential approval of two orally available weight loss drugs may be boosting Metsera's share price too.

Analysis - Metsera Appears To Have Decent All Round Credentials - But Tougher Tests Are Coming

Many of Metsera's management team formerly worked at The Medicines Company prior to forming Metsera. The Medicines Company was acquired by Swiss Pharma giant Novartis (NVS) for $9.7bn in 2019, primarily to gain access to inclisiran, a small interfering RNA (siRNA) therapy for LDL-C (bad cholesterol) reduction, which is now approved as Leqvio, earning >$750m of revenues in 2024.

According to Metsera's 2024 10K:

While at The Medicines Company, key executives on our team designed and carried out more than 20 large-scale clinical trials that enrolled an aggregate of more than 90,000 patients, resulting in the commercial launch of eight drugs, including Angiomax and Leqvio, and 25 peer-reviewed publications in leading medical journals.

Metsera points to the fact that Wegovy/Zepbound users often put weight back on as soon as they stop using them, that titration is complex, tolerability problematic, and that going forward, drugs will be used in combinations more and more, and patients will have the right to migrate the therapy that suits them best.

Metsera also shares the following table, suggesting its drugs have a substantially longer half-life than rivals:

As we can see, MET-097i appears to have a substantially longer half-life than, e.g., tirzepatide - Zepbound - semaglutide - Wegovy, Lilly's "triple-G" therapy retatrutide (so called because it targets glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide, or "GIP," Glucagon-like peptide, or "GLP," and glucagon) Novo's cagrilinitide - Cagrisema - Viking Therapeutics' VK-2735, and is comparable to Amgen's MariTide.

We may note that, going forward, biotechs and pharma may resort to more and more obscure data and theses that suggest their drugs are uniquely well positioned to treat patients, while ultimately, any drug that can't match Wegovy or Zepbound for mean weight loss will likely be considered a second-rate therapy.

For example, Novo promised that Cagrisema would achieve mean 25% weight loss in its clinical study, and the Pharma reported it had "only" achieved 15.7%, Wall Street panic sold, and wiped tens of billions from its market cap valuation in response.

With that said, can the FDA deny approval to a drug with a uniquely differentiated MoA, if it clearly demonstrates positive weight loss credentials in clinical studies, even if the mean weight loss figure does not quite match Zepbound or Wegovy?

I suspect that Metsera (and analysts) are correct in assuming that the FDA will approve multiple GLP-1 drugs in the hope that patients may have more choice, and find a drug - or combinations of drug - that suits them best, and potentially, to prevent Lilly and Novo establishing a duopoly in a forecast $200bn market.

The above table shows all Metsera candidates and data timelines. A positive set of Phase 2b data MET-097i has the potential to triple the share price overnight, as was the case with, e.g., Viking Therapeutics (VKTX) last year.

On the downside, no company to date has made a strong case that their candidate can match the safety and efficacy of currently approved GLP-1s, and therefore we may speculate Metsera, while having the potential to deliver astronomical gains for retail investors, may simply burn through investors' cash without delivering a "knockout" candidate. The current cash position - as of the end of 2024 - is $352.4m, while the net loss in 2024 was $(209.1m).

Only time will tell, but the monthly preliminary readout due ~Q425 looks to be a key catalyst, in my view, while Met233i, the amylin analog, could also drive substantial gains if it steals a march over Novo's amycretin, which achieved 24% placebo adjusted weight loss in a recent study, or Structure's ACCG-2671, which is frankly a much longer shot, its Phase 1 having not yet initiated.

Concluding Thoughts - A Must Add For Your Watchlist, But Approach With Caution

Metsera is an intriguing new entrant in the race to secure approval for more GLP-1 receptor agonists, to challenge the supremacy of tirzepatide and semaglutide. These molecules are also approved to treat Type 2 Diabetes ("T2D"), as Mounjaro/Ozempic respectively, earnings a combined >$20bn in this indication last year.

GLP-1 drugs are an extraordinary phenomenon that are beginning to show their mettle in new indications, from cardiovascular health, to sleep apnea, metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis ("MASH"), Chronic Kidney disease, and potentially even Alzheimer's.

These drugs are self-evidently not easy to develop successfully, hence why Novo and Lilly's market cap valuations and share prices have exploded in recent years, even if Novo's has been suffering off late.

Metsera's challenge is therefore a tough one, but there is an experienced management team in place with experience of securing past drug approvals, several candidates all directed against familiar targets, yet with some key differentiations (dosing regime, longer half-lives, combination therapies), and some intriguing near-term catalysts.

As such, if you are at all interested in the GLP-receptor agonist phenomenon - and if you are a pharma or biotech investor, you absolutely should be - then Metsera is a must add to your watchlist.

I'd stop short of calling Metsera, Inc. a "must-buy," unless you are happy making speculative bets with money you can afford to lose, but a positive Phase 2b for the lead candidate would significantly de-risk the investment opportunity, allowing investors to buy in at a much higher price, but in the expectation of bona fide, further value accreting, approval shot.

If the Phase 2b study fails, the entry price will get much cheaper, but the risk will skyrocket. This ought to be an interesting company to watch across the remainder of 2025, and hopefully, in the longer term also.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: From Weather Woes To Winning Strategies: Texas Roadhouse's Q1 Story
data.description:
Introduction

Texas Roadhouse (NASDAQ:TXRH) often flies under the radar in a market where all the hype usually gravitates around AI companies. After all, it is just a restaurant chain that sells beef for dinner. And yet, the company has proven over and over that it can keep growing at a double-digit annual rate.

Nonetheless, several macroeconomic indicators depict a scenario that pressures Texas Roadhouse. In particular, we are seeing that beef, a key commodity for Texas Roadhouse, has been increasing in price at a brisk pace in late 2024 and early 2025. At the same time, the consumer sentiment index is trending down, and this can constitute a headwind for Texas Roadhouse. On the other side, wage growth is moderating, although it is still higher than inflation (4.3%). Restaurants are particularly sensitive to wage growth because they operate on thin margins.

At the same time, we should counterbalance a concerning macroeconomic environment with a rather simple observation. At any given historical moment, there are reasons to be worried and reasons not to be. Great companies, however, usually thrive, no matter what happens around them. Texas Roadhouse, for example, has managed to strengthen its customer loyalty and is constantly among the top restaurants when considering comparable sales and traffic. Although its industry is highly competitive, Texas Roadhouse distinguished itself from the rest thanks to its outstanding operating efficiency and pleasant experience that customers appreciate. In other words, execution is key for consistent returns.

There is also something quite interesting about Texas Roadhouse's trading history. In the past decade, we have seen it going through several periods of subpar returns (mid-2018-2020; mid-2021 to early 2022; late 2023), followed by rapid price surges. Right now, a pattern is forming that suggests the stock is trending downwards and testing a new bottom before preparing for another move north. As this goes on, the valuation somewhat becomes more interesting because Texas Roadhouse keeps compounding its earnings while the price stabilizes.

Texas Roadhouse Q1 2025 Earnings Review

Since Texas Roadhouse just reported earnings, it is time to take a look at the results and discuss them.

First of all, remember what the company's management said during the Q4 2024 earnings call? It was saying that bad weather was negatively impacting Q1. We will have to see if this was true and what impact this may have had.

Well, total revenue increased 9.6% YoY to $1.45B. Operating income, however, increased only by 1.2% to $134M, and net income was almost flat (+0.4% YoY) to $114M.

This is easily understood: Both food and beverage and labor increased significantly YoY. The former saw a $45M increase (+10%) and the latter increased by $52.5M (+12.3%).

Restaurants must always be gauged through some industry-specific KPIs. In particular, same-store sales growth and restaurant margin dollars are among the most important ones.

Well, same-store sales grew 3.5% YoY, and average weekly sales improved to $163K from $159K last year. Restaurant margin dollars increased 4.7% to $239M (Q1 2024: $228M), but commodity and wage inflation of 2.1% and 4.6%, respectively, made the company's restaurant margin decrease 77 bps.

Now, the same-store sales growth of 3.5% could be disappointing, as Texas Roadhouse has groomed us for a higher print. However, most of this growth happened in March, after severe cold weather stopped and customers returned to Texas Roadhouse's restaurants. In fact, while January and March reported 5.5% and 4.6% comparable sales increases, February only reported 0.5%. As CEO Jerry Morgan disclosed, for March, the company hit an ATHs average weekly sales across its three brands (Texas Roadhouse, Bubba's 33, Jaggers). So, we will have to wait one more quarter to see if there is a true slowdown in same-store sales or not. In any case, most restaurants are currently reporting a decrease in traffic and comparable sales, so being able to grow in a cooling environment is something I view favorably.

Texas Roadhouse confirmed its expansion plan and announced it spent $77.4M in capex, with another $78.3M used for franchise acquisitions. Let me spend a few words on this strategy. Texas Roadhouse operates most of its restaurants (688 out of 792), even though an important piece of the pie is represented by franchised locations (104). But, over time, Texas Roadhouse willingly repurchased franchised locations to integrate them into the whole ecosystem. More importantly, Texas Roadhouse buys already-operating and well-established restaurants, thus being freed from the high initial costs and the operating risk of managing a new opening. At the end of the year, we should see an additional 30 company-owned restaurants.

What about cash flow? Texas Roadhouse reported $238M in operating cash and around $155M spent in capex and acquisitions. This means that the $95M returned to the shareholders via dividends and share repurchases were mostly covered by FCF. Now, in case you are now aware of it, Texas Roadhouse showcases a superb balance sheet, with $220M in cash and no LT-debt. As a result, it has ample room to give out a tiny portion of its cash position to its shareholders, in case the quarterly FCF doesn't fully cover the payment. Moreover, the payout ratio is quite conservative and usually stands around 35% and 40%.

What matters even more when we read a report is the outlook for the rest of the year.

First of all, CFO Chris Monroe reported that in the first five weeks of Q2, same-store sales were up 5%, suggesting the company is in good shape. This increase comes from 3.1% traffic growth and 1.9% check growth (with 2.3% pricing). So, while in Q1 the negative mix was 60 bps, the start of the quarter is 40 bps. Starting Q2, Texas Roadhouse implemented a 1.4% price increase, which will contribute to higher Q2 sales. Therefore, average weekly sales for the first five weeks of Q2 were $164k.

Now, as surprising as it may sound, even Texas Roadhouse will be affected by tariffs. In particular, seafood comes for the most part from non-USMCA countries. Consequently, the company expects commodity inflation to be driven higher for this portion of the overall basket. Moreover, there will be tariffs on disposable and plateware, even though the company expects higher costs to be visible only in the second half of the year. This is why we see the FY guidance for commodity inflation to be around 4%, which is well above the current inflation rate.

Here we could have a problem: if the company guides for 4% inflation, why is it taking only a 1.4% price increase? Michael Bailen, Head of IR, explained once again how this is the typical customer-friendly policy that Texas Roadhouse follows: it doesn't price for commodity inflation, but slowly hikes prices step by step, making sure its customers always perceive the value proposition of its restaurants. So far, this has repaid the company handsomely, with continued traffic growth. Moreover, the company usually takes into account structural inflation, showing how it is long-term focused.

Texas Roadhouse's Valuation

Texas Roadhouse's stock price jumped after earnings by a couple of percentage points. Currently, the annual EPS estimate of $6.58 seems confirmed, and chances are the company will beat consensus. Personally, I expect $6.70. This is a fwd PE of 26, which positions Texas Roadhouse among the most expensive restaurants. The fwd EV/EBITDA is 16.12 and the P/FCF is 15. However, when we consider its growth rate forecasted to be above 10% for the next five years, we have a PEG ratio of 0.6. This positions Texas Roadhouse as much more fairly valued, if not even undervalued (fwd 2030 PE of 15).

As far as I see it, the company's growth story is intact, if not projected to improve, thanks to more advanced kitchens, a larger number of restaurants, and a proven track record of outstanding commitment to operating efficiency.

Considering the stock seems to be having a sort of breather, I consider we are in accumulation territory and rate the stock as a buy.

data.title: Hard To Imagine Retirement Income Portfolio Without These 2 Picks
data.description:
When thinking about retirement (or financial independence) and the notion of living off from the passive income streams, the immediate thing that comes into mind is that it requires a lot of capital. And a lot of capital means many years of sacrificing consumption and being extremely consistent on the savings front.

A common approach to shorten the time-horizon or relax the savings agenda for hitting the required target is to deploy capital into high yielding securities. This would then enhance the monthly or quarterly investment amount that initially consists primarily of the salary saved. Another important but not as "financially justified" benefit from tilting the investment into higher yielding securities is the emotional satisfaction that comes with seeing tangible results (i.e., cash income) manifesting themselves from the hard-earned capital deployed. For some people, including myself, it helps remain motivated and committed to the long journey of gradually building up the necessary amount of capital.

Now, unless we are talking about excessive risk taking and willingness to speculate, the average range of yields that can be accessed through relatively prudent products is between 6% and 9%. Anything above usually introduces unacceptable risk and anything below creates a drag to the income compounding process.

You can correct me if I am wrong, but I would say that the following investment segments explain 80% - 90% of the high-yielding places, where such investors can deploy capital without going too far up in the risk curve:

1. MLP (AMLP) segment which comprises midstream infrastructure players that capture durable and inflation-linked cash flows from long-term take or pay contracts.

2. BDCs (BIZD) which are private credit businesses generating profits from the spreads between cost of capital and private credit yields. This sector is, actually, quite risky, but there are, say, 7 to 10 names that offer sustainable yields.

3. Closed-end funds such as Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA) and the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI). The substance of CEFs is that they invest in relatively stable and defensive assets like real estate, fixed income and preferred securities and then assume a sizeable amount of external leverage to magnify the core holding yield.

4. Real estate (VNQ) and, more specifically, net lease REITs such as Realty Income Corporation (O) and Agree Realty (ADC) that come with investment grade balance sheets, relatively decent yields and inflation-linked cash flows.

5. And then there is a mixture of different sorts of common value-tilted equities that have solid fundamentals but due to, usually, subpar growth prospects trade at low multiples, which mathematically drives up the yield. In this category, the immediate examples that come to my mind are Pfizer (PFE), British American Tobacco (BTI) and Verizon Communications (VZ).

The aforementioned segments account for ~80% of my total portfolio exposure.

However, what these segments do not tackle properly is the following two aspects:

  • Income growth well-above the inflation rate, which over time could compound to something meaningful. This is important also for the diversification angle as there have to be different factor exposures in order to access material dividend growth.
  • Uncorrelated income streams so that, for example, in case the markets go down, the amount of collected income streams would go up.

Let me now share one security example for each of the two aspects above.

#1 - SCHD for income growth

The Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) is a dividend growth focused ETF, which, in my view, does an excellent job in terms of introducing an income growth factor to traditional high-yield portfolios.

The core strategy for SCHD is to invest in 100 U.S. blue-chip businesses that have a combination of robust balance sheets, strong cash flows, decent growth profiles and, importantly, acceptable valuation multiples that render the initial yields attractive.

On a YTD basis, SCHD has dropped by more than 6%, which has opened an opportunity for investors to access above inflation level income growth without making tangible sacrifices on the initial yield end.

The current yield has jumped to 4.0%. By assuming the 5-year dividend CAGR, the implied FWD yield for 2025 is actually closer to 4.5%.

In my opinion, this is not bad given what the real offering of SCHD is.

Historically, SCHD has managed to produce annual income growth of around 10%. The 10-year dividend CAGR is 11.3%, which includes COVID-19 period, inflation-shock, rapidly increasing base rates etc.

We could argue that going forward, SCHD will not be able to sustain this kind of growth, especially against a backdrop of a struggling economy. Yet, if the drop really takes place, I doubt that it will be significant because of the underlying focus on investment grade and strong cash-flowing businesses that face no meaningful valuation pressures (e.g., the portfolio-wide P/CF is ~10x). Plus, SCHD does not invest in such overvalued and no/low dividend paying stocks as Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) and Tesla, Inc. (TSLA).

In a nutshell, I think that SCHD deserves a place in any income-focused portfolio. For investors, who prefer high-yielding securities, the position size in SCHD could be lowered. For example, 5% to 7% of the total exposure that is linked to SCHD should not create any drag to the overall portfolio yield as the current entry yield is quite acceptable - at ~4%.

#2 - JEPI for uncorrelated income

To tackle the second point, I have selected the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI), which is a covered call ETF with the S&P 500 (SPY) as the underlying base.

Before I elaborate on the value-add from integrating JEPI into an income-oriented portfolio, here is a summarized essence of how the ETF works and creates value:

  1. It goes long of the S&P 500.
  2. Then it writes out-of-the-money covered calls against the S&P 500.
  3. The written calls provide option premium income that can be distributed in high dividends.
  4. The drawback of the written calls is that they limit the upside potential for JEPI - i.e., if the S&P 500 surges higher, JEPI will not follow the entire way.

The best-case scenario for JEPI to generate alpha relative to the S&P 500 is when the markets trade sideways and there is an elevated volatility.

In my view, this is exactly the market environment that we have now. The volatility is high, the uncertainty is elevated and the chances for stagnating markets are higher than having rising indices.

If we go back to the subheading or the essence of this aspect - uncorrelated income - then JEPI does the thing through two mechanisms.

First, it collects more cash from selling options when the volatility levels are high. Usually, higher vol means higher risks for traditional income-producing stocks such as BDCs, MLPs, REITs etc. For JEPI, as I said, it brings an opportunity to distribute higher amounts of cash because of options that become more expensive. In fact, this is exactly what we can see by looking at the YTD dividend dynamics. Each month has resulted in higher and higher distributions.

Second, since JEPI sells out-of-the-money options, it still leaves some price upside potential in the ETF. And this is just another element of diversification that introduces a slight bias towards higher multiple (higher growth) stocks that typically are not included in high-yield portfolios.

JEPI at a current yield of 8% is, in my view, a must-have income diversifier in any retirement income portfolio. The key is really the out-of-the-money covered call factor in combination with an underlying exposure to growth-biased companies. So, the job could be done here by some similar ETFs as JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) and the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Core Premium Income ETF (GPIQ) that run covered call strategies, but with different underlying exposures. Personally, I would not allocate more than 5% to these assets to avoid portfolio income volatility (i.e., covered call distribution amounts are per definition volatile).

The bottom line

In this article, I have shared two securities that close the gaps that are usually present in high-yield tilted portfolios.

One gap is the lack of a significant income growth driver in the portfolio. As explained above, SCHD solves this issue nicely without creating any material drag on the overall portfolio yield as the current dividend sits at ~4%.

And the other gap or rather drawback is generally correlated risk and return profiles among the traditional high-yield picks. Here covered call ETF like JEPI come into play very well. JEPI has a high entry yield of ~8% and an income profile that is tied to volatility levels in the market.

data.title: Monster Beverage Earnings Review: Overvalued Despite Strong Market Position
data.description:
The Monster Beverage Investment Thesis

Monster Beverage Corporation (NASDAQ:MNST) is an interesting player in the food market, which attracts a lot of attention due to its excellent long-term performance. However, as the current valuation is relatively high compared to other companies and the growth does not justify this valuation, I still consider the stock to be overvalued after the first quarter results.

So today, I come to the same conclusion I came to two years ago when I wrote that the stock was overvalued. In retrospect, I was right, as the S&P 500 (SPY) is up 36% since then, while Monster is up just ~5%. And I expect the S&P 500 to continue to outperform.

MNST's Q1 2025 Results

After the market closed yesterday, Monster reported first-quarter 2025 results that were worse than the market was expecting. Earnings per share beat slightly by $0.01, but revenue missed by $130 million, which, I think, is not a small miss. The reasons for this are weak sales in the alcohol segment, but also relatively strong currency fluctuations in recent months.

Net sales declined by 2.3% from $1.89 billion to $1.85 billion, but fortunately, gross and operating margins increased over the same period. This suggests that the company's operations have become more efficient. In the earnings call, these improved margins were attributed to pricing actions and supply chain optimization. Especially the point with the price actions seems to be interesting, as this could indicate that Monster still has pricing power and a competitive advantage in the energy drink sector.

If we exclude the alcohol segment and look at sales at constant exchange rates, net sales were up 1.9%, which means that the energy drinks business continues to grow, albeit slowly.

Currency fluctuations in particular distorted results abroad, so that they appear to have declined on a year-on-year basis. But on a currency-adjusted basis, the foreign growth of 6.2% is still satisfactory.

Monster also covers the most important markets in the Western world. Russia, parts of Asia, the Middle East, and Africa are not yet supplied, but in my opinion, these are countries where it can be difficult to do business due to the current political situation. But of course, it is not excluded that in the future, Monster could become active in these countries.

Forecasts for energy drink sales continue to be positive. So the market seems to be growing, which should benefit Monster. However, as with all predictions, it is important to remember that no one can predict the future with 100% certainty, so such predictions should always be taken with a grain of salt.

But it seems that Monster and Red Bull are still the dominant players in the global energy drink market, alternating with market leadership in different countries. In some countries, such as Germany and Austria, Red Bull is the number one, but in others, like Spain, Monster dominates. For example, Monster outperformed Red Bull in the U.K., where it achieved volume growth of 9% in 2024 versus 7.6% for Red Bull. But both are strong growth values.

From a marketing standpoint, Monster continues to sponsor sports that, I think, have a good connection to their target audience, like UFC or MotoGP. However, I think Red Bull still has the edge in terms of global marketing. The soccer teams in the RB universe have a wide reach, as do the numerous motorsports and extreme sports involvements from Red Bull.

Monster's Valuation

Monster's current multiple is around its 5-year average, which means that the stock is relatively fairly valued on a historical basis. But compared to other companies, I think Monster's multiple is too high. Sure, the brand and market dominance, together with Red Bull, play a role in the high multiple. But even that, combined with the growth rates, does not justify the high valuation.

Over the last few years, Monster has experienced the following growth rates:

  • Revenue 3Y: 4.9%
  • Revenue 5Y: 10.5%
  • Net Income: 3Y: 3%
  • Net Income: 5Y: 6%
  • EPS (diluted) 3Y: 4.9%
  • EPS (diluted) 5Y: 7.9%

I think these growth rates are too low for this valuation when there are companies like Meta Platforms (META) today that have the following growth rates but are trading at a lower multiple:

  • Revenue 3Y: 12.4%
  • Revenue 5Y: 18.3%
  • Net Income: 3Y: 21.3%
  • Net Income: 5Y: 26%
  • EPS (diluted) 3Y: 24.6%
  • EPS (diluted) 5Y: 28.5%

Of course, you can say that Monster and Meta are in different industries and therefore the comparison is not valid, but here we can clearly see that you have to pay much less for growth in the market than you do at Monster. I think Monster's valuation has a lot to do with the fact that Monster was one of the best-performing stocks in the S&P 500 over the last 30 years. But those days are over.

Over the last 5 years, the S&P 500 has outperformed Monster, and I expect that to continue as I do not think multiple expansion is very realistic for Monster, and earnings growth also does not currently suggest that Monster will outperform.

MNST' Balance Sheet

The positive thing about the latest results is that I think the current capital side of the balance sheet has improved. Cash and cash equivalents increased from $1.5 billion to $1.9 billion, while inventories decreased. In addition, long-term debt decreased from $373,951 to $199,059, which also speaks to an improvement in the balance sheet.

Conclusion

I still think Monster is a high-quality company that, along with Red Bull, dominates the energy drink market, but the current valuation, combined with the growth rates, is not attractive if your goal, like mine, is to beat the S&P 500 over the long term.

If Monster were trading at a more attractive multiple, I might be interested, but even after the Q1 2025 results, I personally do not see an attractive entry point for me as a long-term investor.
